name: Rwanda
tagline: From the kingdom of the mwami to genocide, reckoning and rebuilding.
summary: Rwanda is a small, densely populated country of the central African highlands whose people — Hutu, Tutsi and Twa — share a single language, Kinyarwanda, a common religion and a centuries-old monarchy. From at least the fifteenth century a kingdom centred on a Tutsi mwami (king) and his clans gradually extended its authority over the hills, reaching its greatest power in the late nineteenth century under Mwami Kigeri IV Rwabugiri. German and then Belgian colonial rule hardened the once-fluid distinction between Hutu and Tutsi into a fixed racial hierarchy, complete with ethnic identity cards, that the colonisers first used to govern through a Tutsi elite and then abandoned. The 1959 Hutu 'Social Revolution' toppled the monarchy and drove tens of thousands of Tutsi into exile; Rwanda became independent of Belgium on 1 July 1962 under Grégoire Kayibanda. Cycles of anti-Tutsi violence, the long rule of Juvénal Habyarimana, and the civil war launched by the exiles' Rwandan Patriotic Front in 1990 culminated, after Habyarimana's plane was shot down on 6 April 1994, in the genocide against the Tutsi: in roughly 100 days an estimated 800,000 to one million people, overwhelmingly Tutsi, were murdered. The genocide ended only with the RPF's military victory in July 1994, and Rwanda has since pursued justice through the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda and the gacaca community courts, reconstruction, and the long, tightly controlled leadership of Paul Kagame.

founding.label: Independence from Belgium
founding.detail: Rwanda became a sovereign republic on 1 July 1962, ending more than four decades of Belgian administration of the trust territory of Ruanda-Urundi (and, before 1916, German rule). A United Nations–supervised referendum on 25 September 1961 had already abolished the monarchy and rejected the reign of Mwami Kigeri V, and the Hutu-led Parmehutu party of Grégoire Kayibanda had won the concurrent elections. At independence Kayibanda became the first president of the Republic of Rwanda, while the sister territory of Urundi became independent as the Kingdom of Burundi on the same day.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 1 July 1962 from Belgium (UN trust territory of Ruanda-Urundi)

quickFacts.label: Precolonial state
quickFacts.value: Kingdom of Rwanda under a Tutsi mwami (king), powerful from the 15th century

quickFacts.label: Colonial rule
quickFacts.value: Germany from the 1890s; Belgium from 1916, mandate/trust territory of Ruanda-Urundi

quickFacts.label: Genocide against the Tutsi
quickFacts.value: April–July 1994; an estimated 800,000 to 1,000,000 killed in roughly 100 days

quickFacts.label: Long-serving leader
quickFacts.value: Paul Kagame, in power since the RPF victory of 1994 (president from 2000)

eras.title: The Kingdom of the Mwami
eras.standfirst: Hutu, Tutsi and Twa shared one language and faith under a centralising monarchy whose Tutsi kings reached their height in the nineteenth century.
body: The earliest inhabitants of the Rwandan highlands were the Twa, a small hunting and foraging population, followed over many centuries by the Hutu, who farmed the hills, and the Tutsi, who herded cattle. All came to speak a single language, Kinyarwanda, to share the same religion and culture, and to be organised into clans that contained Hutu, Tutsi and Twa alike — a pattern that suggests common descent or the long absorption of newcomers rather than separate origins. In precolonial society the central distinction was between cattle-keeping, which carried greater status and power, and cultivation; the terms Tutsi and Hutu came to mark that difference of status, but the line between them was permeable, and a prosperous Hutu could be raised into a Tutsi lineage.

From around the fifteenth century the clans coalesced into small kingdoms, and one of them — ruled by the Nyiginya dynasty of the Tutsi — slowly extended its authority over the others. At its head stood the mwami, a sacred king believed to embody the prosperity of the land, supported by a hierarchy of chiefs who were predominantly, though not exclusively, Tutsi. The kingdom bound lord and client together through cattle contracts such as ubuhake, in which a patron lent cattle in return for service and loyalty — a relationship that offered protection but also entrenched inequality.

By the late eighteenth century a single Tutsi-led state controlled much of present-day Rwanda. Its power peaked in the second half of the nineteenth century under Mwami Kigeri IV Rwabugiri, who reigned from about 1867 to 1895. Through repeated military campaigns and administrative reforms Rwabugiri pushed the kingdom's frontiers toward Lake Kivu and beyond, centralised authority around the throne, and reorganised land and labour in ways that deepened the obligations owed by cultivators to appointed chiefs. He was among the last great rulers of an independent Rwanda; he died in 1895, just as European empires reached the interior of central Africa.

events.title: Clans coalesce into kingdoms
events.summary: Across the highlands, clans containing Hutu, Tutsi and Twa form into small kingdoms; the Nyiginya dynasty of the Tutsi begins to extend its authority, founding the kingdom of Rwanda.

events.title: A centralised Tutsi-led state
events.summary: A single kingdom under a Tutsi mwami controls much of present-day Rwanda, governing through clan chiefs and cattle-clientship arrangements such as ubuhake.

events.title: Reign of Kigeri IV Rwabugiri
events.summary: Mwami Kigeri IV Rwabugiri expands the kingdom through military conquest and administrative reform toward Lake Kivu, marking the high point of precolonial Rwandan power.

events.title: Death of Rwabugiri
events.summary: Rwabugiri dies during a campaign, just as European powers reach the central African interior; a disputed succession leaves the kingdom weakened on the eve of colonial rule.

figures.name: Kigeri IV Rwabugiri
figures.blurb: The great warrior-king under whom the kingdom of Rwanda reached its widest extent and most centralised power, through decades of military campaigns and reforms shortly before colonial rule.

pullquote.text: All Rwandans spoke one language and shared one religion and culture, and their clans contained Hutu, Tutsi and Twa alike.

eras.title: German and Belgian Rule
eras.standfirst: Colonial powers ruling through the monarchy turned the fluid Hutu–Tutsi distinction into a fixed racial hierarchy, sealed by identity cards.
body: Europeans reached Rwanda only in the 1890s, and Germany claimed it as part of German East Africa, governing through the existing monarchy and its chiefs. After Germany's defeat in the First World War, Belgian forces occupied the territory in 1916, and from 1922 Belgium administered Rwanda and neighbouring Burundi together as Ruanda-Urundi under a League of Nations mandate, which became a United Nations trust territory in 1946. Like the Germans before them, the Belgians ruled indirectly, but they intervened far more deeply in Rwandan society.

Colonial administrators and missionaries embraced a pseudo-scientific 'Hamitic' theory that cast the Tutsi as a naturally superior, quasi-foreign aristocracy and the Hutu as a subordinate native majority. The Belgians governed through a favoured Tutsi elite, concentrated chieftaincies in Tutsi hands, and in 1933 introduced ethnic identity cards that fixed every Rwandan as Hutu, Tutsi or Twa — turning a flexible social category into a rigid, inherited and bureaucratically enforced racial label. In 1931 they had deposed Mwami Yuhi V Musinga, whom they found uncooperative, and installed his son Mutara III Rudahigwa.

After the Second World War, as anticolonial and democratic currents spread, Belgium's posture shifted. Faced with Tutsi demands for rapid independence, the colonial authorities and the Catholic Church increasingly sided with the Hutu majority, who in 1957 issued the 'Hutu Manifesto' calling for an end to Tutsi domination. The favouritism that had elevated the Tutsi elite was reversed, but the racial categories the colonisers had hardened remained — now charged with the politics of majority rule, and primed for violence.

events.title: First European contact
events.summary: The German explorer Count Gustav Adolf von Götzen reaches the Rwandan court; Rwanda is incorporated into German East Africa and ruled indirectly through the mwami.

events.title: Belgium occupies Rwanda
events.summary: Belgian forces take Rwanda from Germany during the First World War; from 1922 it is governed with Burundi as Ruanda-Urundi under a League of Nations mandate.

events.title: Belgians depose Mwami Musinga
events.summary: The Belgian administration removes the uncooperative King Yuhi V Musinga and installs his son Mutara III Rudahigwa, tightening colonial control over the monarchy.

events.title: Ethnic identity cards introduced
events.summary: Belgium issues identity cards classifying every Rwandan as Hutu, Tutsi or Twa, fixing a once-fluid social distinction into a rigid, inherited racial category.

events.title: The Hutu Manifesto
events.summary: Hutu leaders publish the 'Hutu Manifesto' demanding an end to Tutsi domination; Belgian authorities and the Church begin to favour the Hutu majority over the Tutsi elite.

figures.name: Mutara III Rudahigwa
figures.blurb: Installed by the Belgians in 1931, the first Rwandan king to be baptised a Catholic; he abolished the ubuhake cattle-clientship and pressed for independence before his sudden death in 1959 on the eve of revolution.

pullquote.text: From 1933 everyone in Ruanda-Urundi was issued with a racial identity card defining them as Hutu, Tutsi or Twa.
pullquote.attribution: On the Belgian classification of Rwandans

eras.title: Revolution, Independence and Exile
eras.standfirst: A Hutu revolution overturned the monarchy, Rwanda became independent in 1962, and recurring violence drove waves of Tutsi into exile.
body: The death of Mwami Mutara III Rudahigwa in July 1959 triggered the upheaval long building under colonial rule. In November 1959 an attack on a Hutu activist sparked a Hutu uprising — the 'Social Revolution' — in which hundreds and then thousands of Tutsi were killed and many homes burned. With Belgian acquiescence, the revolution swept the Hutu political movement to power and shattered the monarchy. The new king, Kigeri V Ndahindurwa, soon fled, and tens of thousands of Tutsi began a long exile in neighbouring countries.

A United Nations–supervised referendum on 25 September 1961 abolished the monarchy, and Grégoire Kayibanda's Parmehutu party won the concurrent elections. On 1 July 1962 Rwanda became fully independent of Belgium, with Kayibanda as its first president and Burundi becoming independent the same day. The new republic defined itself as a Hutu state in which the former order was overturned, and by the time of independence well over a hundred thousand people, mostly Tutsi, had taken refuge abroad.

Independence did not bring peace. Tutsi exiles mounted armed incursions, and each was met with reprisals against Tutsi inside the country: a guerrilla raid in late 1963 was followed by killings that took many thousands of lives. Discrimination was institutionalised through ethnic quotas in schools and employment, and successive waves of refugees swelled the diaspora in Uganda, Burundi, Tanzania and Zaire — a population whose children would later return in arms.

events.title: The Hutu 'Social Revolution'
events.summary: A Hutu uprising, tolerated by the Belgian authorities, kills hundreds of Tutsi and drives many into exile, breaking the power of the Tutsi monarchy.

events.title: Monarchy abolished by referendum
events.summary: A UN-supervised referendum abolishes the monarchy and rejects King Kigeri V; Grégoire Kayibanda's Parmehutu wins the concurrent elections.

events.title: Independence from Belgium
events.summary: Rwanda becomes a sovereign republic with Grégoire Kayibanda as first president, ending Belgian rule of Ruanda-Urundi; Burundi gains independence the same day.

events.title: Reprisals against the Tutsi
events.summary: An incursion by Tutsi exiles is met with mass killings of Tutsi civilians and a further exodus, setting a pattern of cyclical anti-Tutsi violence.

figures.name: Grégoire Kayibanda
figures.blurb: Leader of the Parmehutu and architect of the Hutu 'Social Revolution', he became independent Rwanda's first president in 1962 and ruled until he was overthrown in 1973.

pullquote.text: By independence in 1962 well over a hundred thousand people, primarily Tutsi, had taken refuge in neighbouring states.
pullquote.attribution: On the refugee legacy of the revolution

eras.title: Habyarimana, Civil War and Arusha
eras.standfirst: Juvénal Habyarimana's two decades in power ended in a civil war with the exiles' Rwandan Patriotic Front, a fragile peace, and the eve of catastrophe.
body: In July 1973 the defence minister, Major General Juvénal Habyarimana, seized power in a coup, ending the Kayibanda regime. He founded a single party, the MRND, ruled as Rwanda's unchallenged president for more than two decades, and continued to bar the return of the Tutsi refugees, whose numbers abroad had grown into the hundreds of thousands. His government rested on the same ethnic categories inherited from colonial rule, even as it presented an image of order and development.

On 1 October 1990 the Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF), an army drawn largely from Tutsi exiles based in Uganda, invaded northern Rwanda, opening a civil war. The Habyarimana government responded with propaganda branding all Tutsi as RPF accomplices, with arrests and massacres of Tutsi civilians, and with the arming of militias. Hate media — above all the radio station RTLM — and extremist circles within the regime began openly to prepare for the elimination of the Tutsi.

International pressure produced the Arusha Accords, signed in Tanzania in August 1993, which promised a ceasefire, a power-sharing transitional government and the return of refugees, and brought a small UN peacekeeping force, UNAMIR. But hardliners around the president rejected the settlement and secretly drew up lists and plans for genocide. On the evening of 6 April 1994 the aircraft carrying Habyarimana and the president of Burundi was shot down as it approached Kigali, killing all aboard — the spark that extremists used to launch the killing.

events.title: Habyarimana seizes power
events.summary: Defence minister Juvénal Habyarimana overthrows President Kayibanda in a coup and establishes a one-party state under the MRND.

events.title: RPF invasion begins the civil war
events.summary: The Rwandan Patriotic Front, composed largely of Tutsi exiles from Uganda, invades northern Rwanda, igniting a civil war and intensifying official anti-Tutsi propaganda.

events.title: Arusha Accords signed
events.summary: Government and RPF sign the Arusha peace agreement promising power-sharing and refugee return; a UN force (UNAMIR) deploys, but extremists reject the deal and plan genocide.

events.title: Habyarimana's plane shot down
events.summary: A surface-to-air missile downs the plane carrying President Habyarimana and Burundi's president over Kigali, killing all aboard; extremists seize on it to unleash the genocide.

figures.name: Juvénal Habyarimana
figures.blurb: Seized power in 1973 and ruled for over two decades; his refusal to allow refugees to return helped provoke the civil war, and his death when his plane was shot down on 6 April 1994 triggered the genocide.

pullquote.text: The assassination was taken by extremist leaders of the Hutu majority as the signal to launch a carefully planned campaign to wipe out the Tutsi minority.

eras.title: The Genocide against the Tutsi
eras.standfirst: In roughly 100 days an estimated 800,000 to one million people, overwhelmingly Tutsi, were murdered in a planned campaign of extermination.
body: Within hours of the plane crash on the night of 6 April 1994, the killing began in Kigali. The Presidential Guard, army units and the Interahamwe and other Hutu militias set up roadblocks, consulted prepared lists, and murdered Tutsi and moderate Hutu political figures; the violence spread across the country within days. Identity cards that recorded a person's ethnicity — the legacy of colonial classification — were checked at the barriers and used to single out Tutsi for death.

The genocide was not a spasm of chaos but an organised campaign directed by extremists who had seized control of the state. Radio stations, above all RTLM, incited and coordinated the slaughter, naming victims and urging Hutu to kill their Tutsi neighbours. Massacres engulfed homes, hospitals, schools and churches where terrified families had sought refuge; an estimated 150,000 to 250,000 women were raped. Over roughly 100 days, from April to July 1994, an estimated 800,000 to one million people, the overwhelming majority of them Tutsi, were killed — one of the swiftest mass exterminations of the twentieth century.

The international community, including the small UN force on the ground, did not intervene to halt the killing, and the genocide ended only by force of arms. As the massacres unfolded, the Rwandan Patriotic Front, led by Paul Kagame, resumed its offensive from the north. The RPF captured Kigali and took control of most of the country by mid-July 1994, bringing the genocide to a close and ending the genocidal government — even as a vast wave of Hutu, fearing reprisals, fled across the borders.

events.title: The killing begins
events.summary: Within hours of the plane crash, the Presidential Guard and Interahamwe militias begin murdering Tutsi and moderate Hutu in Kigali, using roadblocks and prepared lists; the violence spreads nationwide.

events.title: Genocide against the Tutsi
events.summary: Over roughly 100 days an estimated 800,000 to one million people, overwhelmingly Tutsi, are systematically murdered, and an estimated 150,000–250,000 women are raped.

events.title: RPF victory ends the genocide
events.summary: Paul Kagame's Rwandan Patriotic Front captures Kigali and most of the country by mid-July, defeating the genocidal regime and halting the mass killing.

figures.name: Paul Kagame
figures.role: Commander of the RPF; later president of Rwanda
figures.blurb: A Tutsi refugee raised in Uganda, he led the Rwandan Patriotic Front whose 1994 offensive ended the genocide; he became Rwanda's de facto leader and, from 2000, its president.

pullquote.text: From April to July 1994, in just over 100 days, more than 800,000 members of the Tutsi minority were systematically murdered.
pullquote.attribution: UN Outreach Programme on the 1994 Genocide against the Tutsi in Rwanda

eras.title: Aftermath, Justice and Reconstruction
eras.standfirst: From a shattered country came a refugee crisis, an international tribunal, the gacaca courts, reconstruction and the long, tightly controlled rule of Paul Kagame.
body: The end of the genocide left Rwanda devastated and triggered one of the largest refugee crises of the era: well over a million Hutu, including many génocidaires, fled to camps in eastern Zaire (now the Democratic Republic of the Congo) and other neighbouring states. A government of national unity took office in Kigali, with Pasteur Bizimungu as president and Paul Kagame as vice-president and defence minister and the effective centre of power. The presence of armed militias in the camps drew Rwanda into successive wars in the Congo from 1996, conflicts that would cost millions of lives across the region.

Reckoning with the scale of the crimes required justice on an unprecedented scale. In November 1994 the UN Security Council created the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR), seated in Arusha, Tanzania, which over two decades indicted 93 individuals and rendered landmark judgments establishing that the 1994 killings constituted genocide. Because the national courts could not try the vast number of accused, Rwanda revived a traditional community justice system, the gacaca courts, which from 2002 until their closure in June 2012 heard more than 1.9 million cases at the local level.

Under Kagame — who became president in 2000 and has won successive elections by overwhelming margins — Rwanda has rebuilt with notable economic growth, public-health gains and a sweeping policy of national unity and reconciliation that bans appeals to ethnicity. The same period has drawn sustained criticism over the suppression of dissent, restrictions on political opposition and the press, and constitutional changes that could keep Kagame in office into the 2030s. Rwanda today commemorates the genocide each April and presents itself as a stable, ambitious state, even as questions about democracy and the costs of order remain.

events.title: Refugee crisis and the Congo wars
events.summary: Over a million Hutu flee to camps in Zaire and beyond; the presence of armed génocidaires there draws Rwanda into the Congo wars from 1996, which kill millions across the region.

events.title: International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda
events.summary: The UN Security Council establishes the ICTR in Arusha, which indicts 93 people and delivers landmark genocide convictions before closing in 2015.

events.title: The gacaca community courts
events.summary: Rwanda revives the traditional gacaca courts to try genocide cases locally; by their closure in June 2012 they have heard more than 1.9 million cases.

events.title: Kagame becomes president
events.summary: Paul Kagame formally assumes the presidency and consolidates power, presiding over reconstruction and growth alongside tight control of political life.

figures.name: Paul Kagame
figures.blurb: Leader of the RPF that ended the genocide and Rwanda's dominant figure ever since; credited with reconstruction and development but criticised for authoritarian rule and the repression of dissent.

pullquote.text: By their closure in 2012 the gacaca courts had tried more than 1.9 million cases — the most comprehensive post-conflict justice programme in the world.

figures.name: Kigeri IV Rwabugiri
figures.blurb: The last great precolonial king, under whom the kingdom of Rwanda reached its widest extent and most centralised power before the arrival of European empires.

figures.name: Grégoire Kayibanda
figures.blurb: Leader of the Hutu 'Social Revolution' and the Parmehutu party who became independent Rwanda's first president in 1962 and ruled until his overthrow in 1973.

figures.name: Paul Kagame
figures.blurb: Led the Rwandan Patriotic Front whose 1994 victory ended the genocide against the Tutsi, and has governed Rwanda ever since, as president from 2000.
